data.title: Raymond James Sees an Attractive Setup in These 2 ‘Strong Buy’ Stocks
data.description:
As December draws to a close and Christmas arrives, investors are pausing to look back and assess a year that never followed a simple script. Policy uncertainty lingered, and trade headlines remained unpredictable as President Trump continued to keep markets guessing. And yet, corporate earnings held up and equities proved surprisingly resilient.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks This Holiday Season

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That staying power is exactly what stood out to Raymond James Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam, who sees 2025 as a reminder of how adaptable markets can be.

“Despite heightened policy uncertainty and persistent geopolitical tensions, both proved remarkably resilient. Strong corporate earnings and AI enthusiasm propelled the S&P 500 to its third consecutive year of double-digit gains, while slowing – but still positive – economic growth and timely Federal Reserve rate cuts pushed Treasury yields lower,” Adam noted.

With the calendar about to flip, Raymond James analysts are turning their focus to what comes next, identifying stocks they believe are well-positioned for 2026. Here are two names that stand out – and they’re not flying solo. According to TipRanks’ database, both carry a Strong Buy consensus from analysts across Wall Street.

Lexeo Therapeutics (LXEO)

Kicking off Raymond James’ list is Lexeo Therapeutics, a clinical-stage biotech taking aim at some of medicine’s toughest challenges by going straight to their genetic roots. The company is developing gene therapies for inherited cardiovascular diseases and APOE4-associated Alzheimer’s, betting that correcting the underlying biology can change the course of these conditions rather than just manage symptoms.

Lexeo’s approach centers on using adeno-associated viruses (AAVs) to deliver therapeutic genes directly into affected cells, effectively using the virus as a transfer agent to put the treatment precisely where it is needed.

The company’s leading program is LX2006, an AAV-based gene therapy designed to treat Friedreich’s ataxia cardiomyopathy by delivering a functional frataxin gene via intravenous dosing. In Phase I/II studies, LX2006 has shown encouraging interim results, including reductions in left ventricular mass index (LVMI), an early indicator of meaningful cardiac impact in this patient population.

Based on the Phase I/II results, the FDA indicated in October that it is open to an accelerated approval pathway, including discussions around a Biologics License Application for LX2006. Pivotal data are expected in 2027, and Lexeo plans to initiate the registrational study in the first half of 2026, pending finalization of the trial protocol.

In addition to LX2006, Lexeo has LX2020 at the Phase I/II stage. This drug candidate is under study for the treatment of PKP2-ACM, by delivering a functional PKP2 gene to cardiac muscle. This drug already has Orphan Drug and Fast Track designations from the FDA. A substantive update on this drug candidate is expected in January.

For Raymond James analyst Christopher Raymond, this combination of advancing clinical data, regulatory momentum, and renewed investor interest in gene therapy underpins a constructive outlook for Lexeo as 2026 comes into view.

“With gene therapy as a class seeing a bit of a renaissance, we think Lexeo’s setup and data to date is worth a closer look… We think Lexeo’s lead asset LX2006 is well-positioned for success in its pivotal trial, based on strong results from two Phase 1/2 clinical trials and constructive feedback from FDA. While pivotal data from this program is not anticipated until 1H27, we anticipate the stock will move next year on a finalized pivotal protocol with FDA and initial POC data on a second, unmodeled gene therapy asset – LX2020 for PKP2-ACM. We model LX2006 revenue of $14.1M, $107.1M, $255.5M, $418.1M, $599.3M and $805.3M for FY27-32, respectively,” Raymond wrote.

To this end, Raymond puts a Buy rating on LXEO shares and complements that with a $25 price target that implies a ~137% upside for the coming year. (To watch Raymond’s track record, click here)

That bullish view is shared across the Street. LXEO carries a unanimous Strong Buy consensus rating, with all 9 analysts rating the stock a Buy. The stock is currently priced at $10.56, and its $20.25 average price target points to a ~92% gain in the next 12 months. (See LXEO stock forecast)

data.title: Insiders Step in on These 2 ‘Strong Buy’ Stocks — And Analysts Are on the Same Page
data.description:
One of the clearest signals retail investors can watch comes not from headlines or hype, but from corporate insiders themselves. Executives and board members don’t trade on whispers – they trade on record. Every purchase is documented, public, and impossible to spin. So when insiders reach into their own pockets to buy shares, it’s a signal worth paying attention to.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

Insiders live inside the business – they see demand trends forming, cost pressures easing or worsening, and strategic moves well before they appear on earnings calls. While in some cases their vision might be narrow, it can also be unusually sharp. And unlike outside investors, they carry direct responsibility for execution, reputation, and shareholder outcomes. That tends to make insider buying selective, deliberate, and tied to moments when confidence outweighs uncertainty.

Regulators make sure these transactions are disclosed, turning what could be an unfair edge into a public signal. Where things get especially interesting is when insider buying lines up with outside conviction – Strong Buy ratings, supportive analyst commentary, and improving fundamentals. When both sides of the wall lean the same way, it can be a serious signal that goes far beyond just hype.

Against this backdrop, we’ve opened up the Insiders’ Hot Stocks tool at TipRanks to look up two stocks that show exactly that profile. Let’s give them a closer look.

Navan (NAVN)

Few areas of corporate operations are as fragmented as business travel and expenses, and that’s exactly the problem Navan set out to solve. Based in Silicon Valley, the company has built a unified platform that brings travel booking, expense management, payments, and cost control into one place. The formula has resonated with large enterprises, including customers such as Pinterest and Lyft.

Navan’s platform is described as more than just a booking tool, but an all-in-one, one-stop shop for business travel needs. Companies can tap into a global inventory of airlines and hotels, including major carriers like Delta, United, and Air France, as well as smaller carriers like RyanAir and Easy Jet, supplemented by such names as Marriot hotels and Budget car rentals. The platform also allows users to select their hotels and control their hotel spending, keeping their travel expenses on target.

This tech firm was founded in 2015, and in October of this year it took the leap and went public through an IPO. Navan put an aggregate total of 36,924,406 shares on the market, a figure that included 6,924,406 sold by existing shareholders. The offering closed on October 31 and generated ~$923 million in gross proceeds, including shares sold by existing shareholders.

On the insider track, we find that Anre Williams, from the Board of Directors, made a purchase of 100,000 shares on December 17. Williams was appointed to the Board in August of this year, and the December share purchase cost him $1.27 million.

Navan announced its fiscal 3Q26 results earlier this month, its first quarterly report as a public company. The company had revenues of $195 million, up 29% year-over-year, and beat the forecast by $13 million. At the bottom line, Navan reported a non-GAAP EPS of 14 cents, 31 cents per share better than expected and a sharp turnaround from the 31-cent EPS loss reported in the prior-year period.

For Morgan Stanley analyst Chris Quintero, the keys here are Navan’s business momentum and its potential for upside. He writes, “The business momentum is clearly building and management’s execution has been strong out of the gate. With the stock now trading down to 3X CY27 Sales after hours, well below peers at 5X, we believe this dislocation presents an attractive opportunity to own a category-leading 20%+ grower. As we had previewed in our initiation, there was a solid amount of upside to the IPO model’s estimate for Q3 and we continue to believe there will be upside to Q4’s guidance, which should act as a catalyst for the stock to re-rate toward our new price target of $20 as our estimates move higher.”

Those views underpin Quintero’s Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating, with his $20 target implying ~26% upside over the next year. (To watch Quintero’s track record, click here)

Zooming out, Wall Street’s conviction looks even stronger. All 11 analysts covering Navan are bullish, giving the stock a unanimous Strong Buy consensus. With shares trading at $15.90, the Street’s $23.64 average price target points to a ~49% gain over the next 12 months. (See NAVN stock forecast)

data.title: These 3 Beaten-Down Software Stocks Could Be Setting Up for a Rebound, Says Analyst
data.description:
Since late 2022, the big story in tech has been artificial intelligence. Generative and agentic AI didn’t just capture headlines – they have been the main driver of market gains and the booster rocket for the tech sector more broadly.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

But the gains have not been evenly spread, and not every tech company has been reaping the rewards. Post-COVID, the software sector, especially the SaaS segment, saw a slump, and hit bottom in late 2022 – just as AI was gearing up. Investors since then have focused on AI, with the obvious result: software hasn’t quite recovered, and the segment has underperformed in 2025.

For software companies, the question now is whether they can successfully pivot their products and business models toward an AI-first future. The sector has faced a similar crossroads before during the shift to cloud computing, and history shows that not everyone makes it through the transition.

BTIG analyst Nick Altmann has been tracking this evolution closely – and in select corners of the software space, he sees reasons to be constructive.

“While software stocks have been pressured, our analysis suggests the fundamentals tell a different story. Measuring the ‘true’ underlying & forward-looking growth across our coverage (TTM sub. Billings, ARR, etc.) shows relatively stable growth over the last ~2 years while FCF margins have continued to improve. And other metrics (i.e. NRR) are actually showing reacceleration across a number of names in our coverage… With valuations attractive and a number of companies already having blessed CY26E estimates, we believe software stocks can have a rebound year in CY26,” Altmann opined.

Putting this view into concrete terms, Altmann has recommended three beaten-down software stocks as Buys for the coming months. They are headed for a rebound, he says; and we can take a look at the TipRanks data, and his comments, to decide if these software names are right for today’s market.

OneStream (OS)

OneStream, the Michigan-based enterprise software firm, with a focus on the financial services sector, is the first software stock we’ll look at here. The company bills itself as ‘the operating system for modern finance,’ and offers its customers a cloud-based AI platform that lets its users reconcile data and manage workarounds, freeing up time and energy for their more vital business functions.

OneStream boasts that its finance platform is unique in the industry. It features embedded AI, as an automation tool, and unifies financial and operational data. The system is adaptable, capable of evolving to meet the changing needs of each customer’s business. The company states that its platform brings quantifiable advantages, including a 54% time reduction in closing books, a 72% reduction in data management cycles, and a 75% improvement in reporting cycles.

Those capabilities – to adapt, and to bring measurable gains – are a major selling point for OneStream, and the company currently has over 1,700 customers on its books, served by over 1,600 employees. The company has operations in more than 45 countries – it has a presence in the Americas, Europe, and the Asia-Pacific region.

We should note here that OneStream’s stock has been falling this year. The shares are down approximately 36% for the year-to-date, even as revenues and earnings have been trending upwards.

A look at the last earnings report, which covered 3Q25, brings more detail to the earnings and revenues. The company reported revenue of $154.3 million, up 19% year-over-year and almost $6 million better than had been expected. The bottom line, the non-GAAP EPS, came to 8 cents, beating the forecast by 6 cents per share. And, also of interest, the company’s free cash flow jumped year-over-year, from $1.3 million to $4.8 million.

Watching OneStream from BTIG, Nick Altmann sees plenty of potential for growth here, writing of the company, “Despite shares underperforming YTD, OS is set to grow >20% in FY25E, and a large, under-digitized TAM (~$50B) provides OS with significant runway to drive continued growth durability over the MT. We believe investors remain concerned over potential disruption to application software from GenAI, though we see the Office of the CFO as more insulated vs. other areas of apps and see OS’ platform as well-positioned to drive further digitization in the office of the CFO given its data-centricity (increasingly important), extensible architecture, and broadening AI solution set.”

The analyst goes on to explain why this stock should make a good portfolio addition, adding to the above, “Moreover, we remain upbeat on OS’ opportunity to capture market share within the Commercial segment, and our checks suggest its new CPM Express product solves down-market pain-points, and we see an opportunity for greater Commercial bookings activity to help not only formulate a more durable growth profile but also provide better bookings uniformity as Enterprise transformation remains lumpy. Lastly, while we remain encouraged on OS’ opportunity to drive continued top-class growth, a rapidly improving FCF profile should provide valuation support. As such, we see a compelling risk vs. reward profile in both the near term and the medium term…”

These comments back up Altmann’s Buy rating on the shares, while his $25 price target implies a one-year gain of 37.5%. (To watch Altmann’s track record, click here)

OneStream’s shares are selling for $18.18, and the $28.27 average target price indicates a 55.5% gain for the coming year. The stock’s Strong Buy consensus rating is based on 14 reviews, including 13 Buys and 1 Hold. (See OS stock forecast)

data.title: Seeking Up to 16% Dividend Yield? Analysts Point to 2 Dividend Stocks Worth a Look
data.description:
With less than two weeks left in 2025, it’s time to start thinking about next year’s portfolio composition. Every investor has a game plan – and while there are countless ways to succeed in the market, dividend investing remains one of the most popular approaches.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

Many investors view dividend stocks purely as a defensive play, useful for preserving capital and generating steady income. But the best dividend stocks can do more than just cushion volatility. Alongside reliable payouts, some come from financially strong companies with durable business models, giving investors the potential for capital appreciation over time. That combination of income, stability, and measured growth means dividends don’t just protect a portfolio – they can enhance total returns.

Keeping this in mind, we’ve dipped into the TipRanks database to look up a couple of stocks that offer strong dividends – including one that yields as high as 16%. That’s a potential return that deserves a closer look, so here are the details.

Invesco Mortgage Capital (IVR)

We’ll start in the world of real estate investment trusts, or REITs. These companies are known as dividend champs – under most tax codes, they get favorable treatment if they return a certain percentage of earnings directly back to their investors. Invesco Mortgage Capital, a typical company in this niche, focuses its activities on financing and managing a range of mortgage-related assets, particularly mortgage-backed securities. The company invests in these assets with the goal of generating sound returns for shareholders.

This company is an externally managed REIT, affiliated with and advised by Invesco Advisers, Inc., an investment adviser with a global footprint and acting as a subsidiary of Invesco, Ltd., a global investment firm with $2.1 trillion in assets under management. These affiliations give Invesco Mortgage Capital a solid backing for its real estate business.

The primary focus of Invesco Mortgage Capital’s business is in mortgage-backed securities that are guaranteed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. The company’s portfolio is well-diversified, with a mix of assets that are secured by both residential and commercial properties and loans. The company holds assets across a broad geographic range and aims for a high dividend yield.

Invesco Mortgage Capital has been paying out quarterly dividends since 2009, delaying only one payment in 2020, during the COVID pandemic – although the company will adjust the payment to match current conditions. The most recent dividend declaration, or a payment of 34 cents per common share, was made in October and the payment was sent out on the 24th of that month. The 34-cent payment annualizes to $1.36 per common share and gives a forward yield of 16.5%.

In its last earnings report, Invesco Mortgage Capital reported a net income per common share of 74 cents, and an ‘earnings available for distribution per common share’ of 58 cents. That latter metric is important, as it directly supports the dividend payment. The company’s net income was a strong turnaround from the 40-cent loss reported in Q2 of this year.

For Compass Point analyst Jason Stewart, this stock presents several reasons for investor optimism. Laying them out, Stewart writes, “Mortgage spreads, the primary driver of book value per share (BVPS), are wider than long-term averages and likely to tighten as fixed income volatility declines and the yield curve steepens. Additionally, political and policy optionality are likely to emerge as a material driver of mortgage asset performance over the next several quarters benefiting MBS holders. Historically, investors have fared well capitalizing on government intervention as non-economic participants in markets. Thematically, investment in the high-quality, liquid agency MBS market at current economic returns is attractive. Increased liquidity in IVR shares against a supportive macro backdrop should result in a modest increase in valuation multiples in conjunction with growth in BVPS from tighter mortgage spreads.” (To watch Stewart’s track record, click here)

The analyst gives IVR shares a Buy rating, along with a $9 price target that implies a one-year upside of 9%. Add in the dividend yield, and this stock’s potential one-year return can exceed 25%. (See IVR stock analysis)

data.title: AMZN and AMD: Cowen Calls These 2 AI Stock Giants Its Best Ideas for 2026
data.description:
So much has happened this year. After a spring correction triggered by President Trump’s aggressive tariff policies, stocks staged a powerful rebound as inflation cooled and markets began pricing in a more accommodative Federal Reserve. At the same time, strong earnings and relentless enthusiasm around AI propelled mega-cap leadership, pushing the S&P 500 to nearly a 16% gain for the year.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

Looking ahead, Cowen’s global head of research, Robert Fagin, sees a constructive backdrop forming as markets head into 2026, even with risks still in play.

“For all the policy shocks and uncertainty in 2025, we are on track to end the year with global GDP growth at 3.0%, 10y Treasuries around 50bps lower, the USD 7% weaker, and the S&P 500 15% higher. Our forecasts for 2026 see fewer shocks, ongoing risks, but much the same outcome: global growth of 2.8%, a further 50bps decline in 10y rates, and a 6% fall in the USD. Central bank accommodation and ongoing fiscal support seem enough to keep risks to more bark than bite,” Fagin opined.

Acting on this outlook, Cowen’s analysts have named two stocks as their “best ideas” for the coming year – Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD), two major forces in AI. According to the TipRanks database, Wall Street rates both stocks as Strong Buys, with double-digit upside potential over the next year. Let’s take a closer look at why Cowen shares that optimism.

Amazon

First up, Amazon is currently the fifth most valuable publicly traded company on Wall Street, with a market cap of $2.38 trillion. Amazon has built itself up to that impressive scale through its dominance in the world of online retail, and through its expressed mastery of shipping and logistics. The company generated almost $640 billion in total revenue last year, and had over $70 billion in cash assets as of September 30 this year.

Amazon hasn’t stood still on its retail business, even though that makes up approximately 80% of its revenue stream. The company is also well-known for its work with AI, cloud computing, and the combination of the two. AWS, Amazon’s subscription cloud platform, is a major revenue generator – with quarterly revenue of well over $30 billion in recent reports – and is also a key driver of Amazon’s AI work. Through AWS, Amazon offers a range of AI-powered features, including automation tools and apps, graphic design tools, autocomplete systems; it’s a long list.

Amazon is also a major collaborator with smaller companies in their own AI forays. Just this month, Amazon announced that the antibody company Proteintech had chosen AWS as its cloud provider of preference – and had used the service to create its industry’s fits AI antibody assistant. On the same day, Amazon also announced that Plaud, the AI conversational intelligence firm, was using AWS – and its AI – to speed up the global deployment of its meeting intelligence solutions. And to round out that day, Amazon made public that AWS had introduced its Graviton 5 processor chip, giving 25% higher performance than the preceding generation.

Along with AWS, Amazon’s retail business is also benefiting from the expansion of AI-powered tools and features. By now, we’ve all seen the commercials for Amazon’s visual shopping features, which are based on AI technology. The company is also using AI to make its e-commerce experience interactive in other ways, with a shopping assistant and improved search bar functionality.

When we turn to Amazon’s last set of financial results, which covered 3Q25, we find that the retail giant generated $180.2 billion for the quarter. This figure represented a solid 13.4% year-over-year increase and beat the estimates by $2.4 billion. At Amazon’s bottom line, we saw an EPS of $1.95, based on a net income of $21.2 billion. Looking at some drill-downs, we see that Amazon’s North American sales reached $106.3 billion, for an 11% y/y increase, while international sales were up 14% to reach $40.9 billion. The biggest gainer, however, was AWS, which had a year-over-year jump of 20% and hit $33 billion in quarterly revenue, or 18.3% of the total.

For Cowen analyst John Blackledge, there are several reasons why Amazon is a ‘top pick.’ Laying them out, the 5-star analyst writes, “AMZN is our top ’26 large cap pick based on three key drivers, incl: i) Accelerating AWS revenue growth: We forecast AWS revenue to accelerate in 4Q25 and accelerate further in both ’26 and ’27 on the heels of strong core and AI workload demand and alongside expanding AI infrastructure capacity… ii) eCommerce and Advertising Momentum: AMZN’s eCommerce biz should see strong growth in ’26 driven by cont’d record delivery speed (which drives purchase conversion), expansion of same day perishables biz, & investment in rural markets. We estimate AMZN’s ’26 Ads rev grows high teens y/y driven by sponsored products, AMZN’s demand-side platform (DSP), and emerging Prime Video ad biz; and iii) Continued Margin Expansion: We forecast AMZN ’26 Op Inc of $104BN, ~4% above consensus, yielding 12.8% Op Inc (GAAP) margins, as high margin Ads and AWS businesses continue ramping while eCommerce margins benefit from lower cost to serve.”

Looking forward from here, Blackledge rates Amazon’s stock as a Buy, with a $300 price target that indicates potential for a 35% upside in the coming year. (To watch Blackledge’s track record, click here)

Amazon’s Strong Buy consensus rating is based on 45 analyst reviews, with a highly lopsided split of 44 Buys to 1 Hold. The shares are currently priced at $222.56 and the $296.85 average price target implies a one-year gain of 33%. (See AMZN stock forecast)

data.title: ServiceNow Stock: Expensive but a Good Fit, Says D.A. Davidson About Potential Armis Acquisition
data.description:
ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) shares appear to be on more of an even keel in Tuesday’s session following Monday’s sharp 11.5% drop. The downturn came in the wake of a Bloomberg report stating that the software company is nearing a deal to acquire cybersecurity start-up Armis for up to $7 billion. If completed, this would mark ServiceNow’s largest acquisition to date, surpassing its $2.85 billion purchase of AI start-up Moveworks, which was announced in March and recently finalized.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

So, investors evidently were not happy with the news, but D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria thinks otherwise. “The acquisition is expensive from a valuation perspective at ~23x ARR, however we view Armis as an attractive target for NOW to bolster its growing suite of Security products,” the analyst opined.

Armis is one of the fastest-growing private security software companies, having recently surpassed $300 million in ARR, reflecting 50% year-over-year ARR growth. In November, the company raised $435 million at a $6.1 billion valuation. Originally founded to secure Operational Technology (OT) and Internet of Things (IoT) environments, Armis has since extended its capabilities to recognize all asset types in an organization with advanced discovery and intelligence features.

Luria reckons that acquiring Armis would increase CY26 growth by at least 50 basis points while reducing FCF margins by approximately 200 basis points. The acquisition has not yet been confirmed, so financing details are unknown; however, given the deal’s size, the analyst expects a 50/50 mix of cash and stock, similar to ServiceNow’s acquisition of Moveworks. If ServiceNow pays $7 billion for Armis with a 50/50 cash and stock structure, the transaction would be dilutive to NOW shares by roughly 200 basis points.

“Overall,” Luria said, “we believe Armis fits well in NOW’s portfolio of Security offerings and see strong potential for both cross-selling and upselling of Armis into NOW’s customer base given Armis is a best of breed platform that provides visibility into all IT assets.”

ServiceNow recently surpassed $1 billion in Security ACV (annual contract value), making Security its fifth product line to reach this level. Luria believes the purchase of Armis would help the company increase market share and “accelerate penetration” into the $45 billion TAM for OT/IoT security. Armis serves a similar customer base as ServiceNow, selling to large enterprises and having had success in the federal sector, financial services, retail, and other industries. However, Luria does think the acquisition carries “integration risk,” as ServiceNow has just completed its acquisition of Moveworks. Historically, ServiceNow has not been highly active in large M&A transactions, although CEO Bill McDermott has more experience with such deals.

All told, Luria reiterated a Buy rating on NOW shares although to account for the potential FCF and share dilution, his price target goes from $1,250 to $1,100. Nevertheless, should the figure be met, investors will be pocketing returns of 41.5% a year from now. (To watch Luria’s track record, click here)

Most analysts are thinking along the same lines; based on a mix of 24 Buys, 4 Holds and 1 Sell, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Strong Buy. Going by the $1,140.84 average price target, shares will appreciate by 48% in the year ahead. (See NOW stock forecast)

data.title: Palantir Stock: Momentum Continues to Build, Says BofA
data.description:
Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) has experienced significant expansion in recent years, but the growth continues to gain momentum alongside the increasing adoption of AI at the enterprise level.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium and Invest with Confidence

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

So says BofA’s Mariana Perez Mora, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, following investor meetings with some of the firm’s management, including CFO Dave Glazer.

Supported by a strengthening backlog, shorter contract durations, and customers moving more quickly up the PLTR value chain, Perez Mora notes that the US Commercial segment “continues to show the strongest momentum.”

Within the Government segment, Palantir recently secured a two-year $448 million US Navy ShipOS contract. The award reinforces Palantir’s positioning alongside the Trump Administration’s push to reindustrialize the US. On December 9, at the Department of the Navy Rapid Capabilities Office Industry Day in Washington, DC, the company and the US Navy announced the agreement to deploy Foundry and AIP across the shipbuilding industrial base. The Shipbuilding Operating System, or ShipOS, builds on earlier pilots from the July 2025 collaboration with BlueForce Alliance, which focused on deploying Palantir’s Warp Speed manufacturing OS to speed up shipbuilding. These efforts draw on Palantir’s expanding manufacturing expertise, particularly in shipbuilding, following its 2022 partnership with HD Hyundai Heavy to implement Foundry at Korea Shipbuilding & Offshore Engineering. “We see the partnership as particularly impactful given the years-long challenges faced by shipbuilders in meeting the Navy’s production targets, including for two Virginia-class submarines annually,” the 5-star analyst said on the matter. The Navy initially focused ShipOS on addressing supply chain bottlenecks in submarine construction but intends to gradually extend it to surface ships, strengthening the maritime industrial base through proactive risk management and cost reductions.

Meanwhile, with no big CapEx needs, Palantir remains focused on investing in product extensions. These upgrades enable more efficient operations, greater interoperability with open-source and other software, faster development cycles, stronger security and governance, low-code/no-code accessibility, and improved product quality. They also support scalability, allowing Palantir to build prototypes in hours and achieve production-ready solutions within weeks. Among the recent product additions are AI FDEs (forward-deployed engineers), which build data transformations and develop applications; Autopilot, which manages transitions between human-driven and automated processes; and MCP, which automates workflows that previously required manual steps.

“While momentum builds,” Perez Mora summed up, “we continue to see opportunity for margin expansion with operating leverage growing with each use case across the businesses.”

Conveying her confidence, Perez Mora maintained a Buy rating on the stock along with a Street-high price objective of $255, suggesting the shares will gain 39% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Perez Mora’s track record, click here)

That’s a bull’s take but most on the Street aren’t quite as confident; based on a mix of 11 Holds, 3 Buys and 2 Sells, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Hold. Going by the $187.87 average target, shares will stay rangebound for the time being. (See Palantir stock forecast)

The primary item to monitor is management’s guidance on when “incremental capacity” begins to come online in a meaningful way and how quickly the product mix can continue shifting toward higher-margin cloud and data center offerings.

As for the 2QF26 guide, that should beat consensus too. Although February is typically a seasonally weaker quarter, Cassidy sees PC and handset OEMs “scrambling” to secure DRAM and NAND Flash supply not just for the quarter, but through CY26 and into CY27. This trend should help balance margins across all segments, including Cloud, Core Data Center, PC & Mobile, and Automotive & Embedded.

The “key debate” continues to center on capacity optimization and timing, with the margin outlook increasingly dependent on execution. Specifically, this hinges on how effectively Micron can optimize existing capacity and convert it into “sellable output.” Recent steps to exit lower-return consumer channels, including the Crucial consumer business, underscore management’s emphasis on “higher-value end markets.” Investors will be closely watching management’s comments on the capacity ramp schedule, specifically the timing of near-term vs. longer-term supply additions, the rate at which usable bit supply increases, and the distribution of that additional output among HBM, server DRAM, and other market segments.

Despite the shares’ strong rally, given the company’s financial performance, Cassidy thinks the growth is justified. The forward P/E remains around 12x, similar to the start of the year. “Meanwhile,” the analyst summed up, “the company is becoming structurally stronger as AI applications are both increasing the need for memory and allowing for differentiation. We recommend owning the stock.”

Quantifying that stance, Cassidy’s Buy rating is backed by a $300 price target, implying the stock will post growth of 26% in the months ahead. (To watch Cassidy’s track record, click here)

Cassidy’s take is a common one on Wall Street right now; the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 26 Buys and 3 Holds. Going by the $266.14 average target, a year from now, shares will be changing hands for a 12% premium. (See Micron stock forecast)

Middleby Corporation (MIDD)

The company operates in three business segments – food service, residential, and food processing – and has more than 120 brands on the market.

Middleby is a $7.5 billion company, and generated $3.88 billion in total revenue last year – a testament to the centrality of food and kitchens in ordinary life. The company’s brands include names such as Burford and Concordia, Marsal and Rangemaster, and have applications in just about every facet of food service, from industrial kitchens to small pizza eateries to home cooking. Middleby employs more than 11,000 people worldwide, at 22 facilities.

The company is in the process of changing its focus, and on December 4 announced that it is selling a 51% stake in its Residential Kitchen business to affiliates of the investment platform 26North Partners LP. The deal will allow Middleby to focus on commercial foodservice equipment as a core business. The transaction is valued at $885 million and is expected to be completed during 1H26.

In its last reported financial quarter, 3Q25, Middleby had revenues of $982.13 million, up 4% year-over-year and nearly $21 million better than had been forecast. The company’s bottom line in the period, the non-GAAP EPS of $2.37, was up 4 cents per share from the prior-year quarter and came in 30 cents per share above the estimates.

For Jefferies analyst Saree Boroditsky, this stock presents a solid way forward, particularly with its turn toward a commercial foodservice focus. She writes, “We are upgrading MIDD to Buy, ahead of a recovery in Commercial Foodservice demand, which we see as the key catalyst for the stock… We see potential for the standalone Commercial Foodservice business to generate double-digit growth from a combination of organic growth / margin expansion and share repurchases (6-8% annually). Based on our operating forecast, the standalone entity could generate >$8.50/share by 2027, with upside to ~$9.80/share if growth aligns with management’s MSD top-line outlook and 40% incrementals.”

data.title: Wedbush Predicts Up to 600% Jump for These 2 ‘Strong Buy’ Stocks
data.description:
After a blistering run, the stock market has lately found itself stuck in a bit of a trading rut, with more and more chatter that the AI boom is drifting from ‘transformational’ toward ‘show-me-the-profits.’ Investors have been zeroing in on ballooning AI capex plans, and the fear is simple – if Big Tech keeps pouring tens of billions into data centers without clear payback, today’s premium valuations start looking shaky. That dynamic has pushed the market toward a rotation out of some AI darlings.

  • Unlock hedge-fund level data and powerful investing tools designed to help you make smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis so your portfolio is always positioned for maximum potential

Against this backdrop, Wedbush analyst Laura Chico argues that while AI remains a powerful long-term theme, the current pause in market leadership is opening the door for other high-beta areas to attract attention. Biotech, she says, is emerging as one of those destinations. The sector’s bellwether, the SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI), is up about 37% year-to-date, ahead of the S&P 500’s 16% gain.

Asking the question, “What’s the setup into 2026?” Chico offers a straightforward answer: “Simply put, we think biotech momentum continues. FDA chaos seems priced in at this point and with the ‘AI’ trade slowing, we see an opportunity for biotech to garner more interest… We see several ideas featuring embedded de-risking, valuations underestimating validated market opportunities, and defined catalysts within the next 12 -18 months.”

Leaning into that opportunity, Chico and her colleagues at Wedbush are pointing out two biotech stocks that bring triple-digit upside potential to the table – in one case, as much as 600% for the year ahead. According to the TipRanks database, both names get a ‘Strong Buy’ rating from the broader analyst consensus, too. Let’s take a closer look at what’s driving the enthusiasm across the board.

Artiva Biotherapeutics (ARTV)

The first stock drawing Wedbush’s attention is Artiva Biotherapeutics, a micro-cap biotech developing a new class of natural killer (NK) cell-based therapies for autoimmune diseases and cancers. These conditions can be devastating for patients and often come with limited or ineffective treatment options. Artiva’s lead candidate, AlloNK, is being developed as a potentially safer, more effective, and more accessible form of cell therapy.

Artiva is evaluating AlloNK across multiple autoimmune indications through several clinical programs, including a Phase 2a basket trial that tests a single therapy across related diseases. That ongoing study is examining AlloNK, in combination with rituximab, in refractory rheumatoid arthritis (RA), Sjögren’s disease (SjD), idiopathic inflammatory myopathies (IIMs), and systemic sclerosis (SSc). The trial received FDA clearance in May, with patient dosing beginning in August. In parallel, Artiva is running a company-sponsored Phase 1/1b trial in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), with or without lupus nephritis, using AlloNK in combination with anti-CD20 antibodies, and an investigator-initiated trial spanning additional autoimmune conditions, including RA, pemphigus vulgaris, SLE, and ANCA-associated vasculitis.

In October, the FDA granted Fast Track designation to AlloNK in refractory RA, elevating it to the program’s lead indication and underscoring its potential to address a significant unmet need among more than 150,000 U.S. patients who have failed at least two biologic or targeted synthetic DMARDs.

More recently, Artiva reported encouraging safety and tolerability data from 32 autoimmune patients treated with AlloNK plus an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody across its clinical programs, as of the October 1 data cutoff. Patients received either 1 billion or 4 billion AlloNK cells per dose, all in outpatient settings, largely at community rheumatology sites. Importantly, no cases of cytokine release syndrome or neurotoxicity were observed, and all patients achieved complete B-cell depletion by Day 13. Artiva plans to share initial clinical response data in refractory RA and engage with the FDA on the design of a pivotal RA trial in the first half of 2026.

With early clinical validation taking shape and shares trading at $3.30, Wedbush analyst Martin Fan sees a compelling setup.

“We are encouraged by the possibility of rapid development in RA due to accelerating enrollment and regulatory support. Upside is likely should ARTV pursue expansion opportunities from other indications in its ongoing Ph 2a basket trial including Sjögren’s disease (SjD), idiopathic inflammatory myopathies (myositis/IIM), and systemic sclerosis (scleroderma/SSc)… We see room for substantial near-term appreciation as sizeable data sets emerge… Shares remain below cash of $5/sh, and we would be buyers ahead of key data and regulatory events,” Fan noted.

So how much upside does Fan see from here? The analyst rates ARTV an Outperform (i.e., Buy), with a $23 price target that suggests a whopping ~600% upside over the next 12 months. (To watch Fan’s track record, click here)

That bullish stance is echoed across the Street. Artiva carries a Strong Buy consensus, backed by 5 unanimously positive analyst ratings, while the average price target of $16.50 implies a potential gain of 400% over the coming year. (See ARTV stock forecast)

data.title: Looking for High-Yield Dividend Stocks? Citizens JMP Suggests 2 Names — One Offers a Massive 13% Yield
data.description:
As we head into 2026, it’s only natural to want to set up the best possible portfolio for the new year. The key here is returns – we’ve seen bullish markets for several years now, and investors want to keep up that momentum. Dividend stocks can make a clear contribution.

Among the higher-yield corners of the market, business development companies (BDCs) stand out. These firms provide financing to small- and medium-sized businesses that often fall outside the traditional banking system, and like REITs, they benefit from favorable tax treatment when they distribute a large share of earnings to shareholders. That leads to high and usually reliable dividends.

Covering the BDC space for Citizens JMP, analyst Brian McKenna notes that despite recent underperformance, the fundamentals remain intact.

“Both the Alts and BDCs have underperformed over the past several months, although as we have written about at length, underlying fundamentals remain generally healthy for many (vs. perceptions across the marketplace today),” McKenna said. “We think the recent underperformance across the industry is largely unwarranted, specifically as the broader markets continue to trade at/near all-time highs, and we see (yet again) another compelling longer-term buying opportunity in these stocks.”

For investors seeking out high-yield dividend payers, this is exactly what is needed: an attractive point of entry and strong dividend. McKenna has two names to offer, including one with a massive yield of 13%. Let’s open up the TipRanks database and give them a closer look so we can find out what else they bring to the table.

Trinity Capital (TRIN)

The first company on our list, Trinity Capital, is an alternative asset manager that is structured for business purposes as an internally managed BDC. The company aims to provide its client firms with access to the credit market, and its investors with stable and consistent returns. Trinity invests its capital in a wide range of target firms, across several categories: tech, equipment, life sciences, sponsor finance, and asset-based lending. Since its founding in 2008, Trinity has poured some $5.1 billion into its investments. The company is based in Phoenix and operates in the US and Europe.

Trinity currently has $2.6 billion in assets under management, and boasts a market cap of $1.15 billion. The company makes careful vetting of its investment targets, keeping in mind its constant goal of maintaining a sound return for investors. This is usually returned via dividend distributions, and as of September this year Trinity has returned a cumulative $411 million through those payments.

The most recent dividend payment was declared on September 17 and paid out on October 15. The payment, of 51 cents per common share, annualizes to $2.04 and gives a forward yield of 13.5%. Trinity has been paying out dividends since 2021, has never missed a quarterly payment, and does have a history of adding special or supplemental dividends when appropriate.

In its last earnings report, covering 3Q25, Trinity reported a total investment income of $75.6 million. This was up more than 22% year-over-year and beat the forecast by almost $1.2 million. At the bottom line, Trinity reported a net investment income of $37 million, which translated to a per-share value of 52 cents. The company’s net investment income was up 26% year-over-year, although the 52-cent per-share value missed expectations by a penny. We should note that the NII-per-share was sufficient to fully cover the last dividend.

For Citizens analyst McKenna, this company offers a combination of growth and a solid dividend. He writes, “Given several company-specific drivers at Trinity, the outlook for earnings growth and ROEs remains healthy. Specifically, we currently model 1% YOY growth in NII per share (and our 2026E NII totals $2.10 per share) and an 15.5% ROE in 2026, which should ultimately drive solid dividend coverage over the next 12+ months (we think TRIN’s dividend coverage ratio troughed in 2025 at ~102%). Thus, we believe the current $0.51 per share quarterly dividend, or $2.04 per share annually (implying a 103% dividend coverage ratio), remains rock solid, equating to an attractive (and elevated) yield of 13.7% (as of writing), during a period when dividend coverage ratios for many in the group are set to decline notably, and in some cases, future dividends are potentially at risk depending on the magnitude of future base rates cuts (among other macro factors)…”

The 5-star analyst sums up with an optimistic outlook: “Given what we view to be a healthy outlook and accelerating momentum into 2026, we expect this strong outperformance will persist moving forward.”

These comments back up McKenna’s Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, while his $17.50 price target points toward a 16% upside in the coming year. Add in the dividend yield, and this stock’s one-year return can exceed 29%. (To watch McKenna’s track record, click here)

Overall, Trinity has earned a Moderate Buy consensus rating from the Street, based on 6 recent reviews that include 5 Buys and 1 Sell. The stock is priced at $15.12 and its $16.50 average price target indicates a one-year gain of 9%. (See TRIN stock forecast)

data.description:
One of the clearest signals investors can watch doesn’t come from charts, price action, or analyst chatter – it comes from the people running the companies themselves. Insider trading often sounds like a shady practice, but in the legal, regulated sense, it simply refers to stock activity by those on the inside: CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior executives with a front-row seat to a company’s financial reality and future direction.

  • Claim 60% off TipRanks Premium for data-backed insights and research tools you need to invest with confidence.
  • Subscribe to TipRanks' Smart Investor Picks and see our data in action through our high-performing model portfolio - now also 60% off

Insiders see what most retail investors can’t – upcoming contracts, product momentum, shifting demand trends, operational improvements, and the health of the balance sheet before it’s reflected in quarterly reports. When they put their own capital to work, particularly in meaningful size, it’s rarely random.

So when insiders start scooping up shares – and doing it in seven-figure chunks – investors pay attention.

With that in mind, we’ve used the Insiders’ Hot Stocks tool at TipRanks to look up the details on two stocks where insiders have recently made major purchases totaling millions. Both carry Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street and offer compelling upside potential based on current price targets.

Let’s break them down and see why both analysts and insiders are lining up behind them.

DoorDash (DASH)

First up is a Silicon Valley tech firm that you’ve probably heard of, DoorDash. The company was founded in 2013, and in the 12 years since it has built itself into a $97 billion giant of the online food ordering and delivery business. The company has operations across the US and in 30 countries around the world, using its online marketplace and commerce platforms to connect customers with their favorite dining options.

While DoorDash has cloaked itself in its reputation as a scrappy startup, the company has been working hard for years to steadily expand its footprint. This year alone, DoorDash spent $1.2 billion in cash to acquire the SevenRooms restaurant booking platform, and followed that with its $3.9 billion acquisition deal for the British food delivery company Deliveroo. Just a few years ago, in 2022, DoorDash completed an even larger, $8.1 billion deal to acquire Wolt in an all-stock transaction.

Looking ahead, DoorDash said in early November of this year that it intends to continue the big spending. The company announced that it will be initiating ‘several hundred million’ in new outlays, for projects like autonomous delivery and improved global tech stacks.

On the former, DoorDash has already begun moving. The company released Dot, its first autonomous delivery robot, in September of this year. The commercial autonomous robot is designed for local delivery, and can handle sidewalks, bike lanes, and driveways. The Dot robot is undergoing an early access program in the Arizona cities of Tempe and Mesa.

Turning to the insiders, we find that Alfred Lin, of the Board of Directors, spent over $100 million in the last week of November buying up two blocks of DASH shares. The two purchases were for 389,047 shares and 125,000 shares, and brought Lin’s total stake in DoorDash to $7.26 billion.

In DoorDash’s 3Q25 report, quarterly revenues were up 27% year-over-year to reach $3.4 billion, and beat the forecast by $89 million. At the bottom line, DoorDash realized an EPS of 55 cents per share, up from the 38 cents reported in 3Q24 but missing the estimates by 13 cents. The company had $7.4 billion cash and other liquid assets on hand at the end of the third quarter.

Despite the overall positive nature of the Q3 report – revenue and earnings were up year-over-year, and cash holdings are strong – the stock fell 17% after the earnings release. The decline marked DASH’s worst-ever trading session, and came as investors were spooked by the announced spending plans. We should note that the stock has started posting gains in recent weeks.

For Evercore analyst Mark Mahaney, DASH presents investors with a solid growth opportunity. He writes of the stock, “We continue to believe that DASH can maintain close to premium rev growth (~20%) thanks to its market leadership position, excellent execution, and its very large TAM, based on our proprietary analysis. We believe that DASH has done a very effective job with its Wolt acquisition, and believe it can repeat the performance with Deliveroo. We believe DASH is likely taking market share in both restaurant and grocery delivery across most of its markets, and our recent Annual Delivery Survey showed DASH being the #1 preference for U.S. grocery delivery for the first time.”

Mahaney sums up his outlook on the stock with some additional detail into its inherent strengths, adding, “Ongoing investments may dampen incremental margins, but EBITDA on a margin and dollar basis should still increase nicely into FY26 and beyond. We see end-market consumer demand for Delivery as resilient, and believe Dasher supply will remain robust (counter-cyclical hedge)… We see DASH as a best-in-class operator, and we view valuation as reasonable.”

Quantifying his stance, the analyst rates DASH shares as Outperform (i.e., Buy), with a $360 price target that indicates room for a 60% gain in the next 12 months. (To watch Mahaney’s track record, click here)

The Strong Buy consensus rating in DASH is based on 30 recent reviews, including 24 to Buy and 6 to Hold. The stock is currently trading for $225.43 and its $284.46 average price target implies a one-year upside of 26%. (See DASH stock forecast)

data.title: Is Carvana Stock Still a Buy Following S&P 500 Inclusion? BofA Weighs In
data.description:
The Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) juggernaut is showing no signs of slowing down, with the week kicking off with another surge for the online used car seller’s shares. The stock was up by 12% as of writing, adding to a year-to-date haul that has crossed 115%. That’s remarkable enough, but seems rather modest when you consider the stock is up by nearly 8,000% since the end of 2022, a time when the company flirted dangerously with bankruptcy.

It’s been one wild turnaround, with the latest uptick based on achieving a significant milestone. As part of its quarterly rebalance, Carvana is about to get added to the S&P 500 index, the inclusion taking effect before the market opens on December 22.

Back in June, BofA analyst Michael McGovern identified S&P inclusion as his “top potential catalyst” for the stock, given the company had already met the profit requirements for several quarters.

The recent run-up, says McGovern, appears to be driven by encouraging third-party data on unit sales, which eased earlier concerns about a potential slowdown. The company sold 156,000 units in Q3 and provided guidance of over 150,000 for Q4, while analysts are now forecasting roughly 155,000 units for the quarter. “We see consumer demand as stable/strong, leading to little deceleration, in part driven by share gains vs. CarMax, as management returns ‘fundamental gains’ to customers via lower pricing and lower APRs,” McGovern said.

The analyst anticipates that Carvana will overtake CarMax in quarterly units sold sometime in 2026. That said, McGovern does point out Google Trends data for “Carvana Sell My Car” still shows a modest decline, with interest peaking in July, and it’s worth noting that trade-in units remain Carvana’s most profitable segment.

Nevertheless, to reflect the recent positive momentum in unit volumes, McGovern has revised his DCF model after previously reducing estimates in line with management’s conservative guide. The analyst now thinks the company can sustain a 20% unit growth CAGR between 2027 and 2032, up from the prior 18.5%. “Although cyclicality remains a risk to that level of growth, the current trajectory of share gains vs. a flat industry underscores the idiosyncratic growth potential,” McGovern went on to say on the matter. Carvana is also benefiting from favorable developments in its capital structure and the potential for a lower cost of capital, thanks to recent credit rating upgrades and its inclusion in the S&P 500.

Accordingly, McGovern reiterated a Buy rating on the shares and raised his price objective from $385 to $455, although the new figure suggests the shares are fully valued. (To watch McGovern’s track record, click here)

It’s a similar story elsewhere on the Street. The stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 16 Buys vs. 3 Holds. However, the $436.56 average target implies the shares will stay rangebound for the time being. It will be interesting to see whether other analysts raise their price targets or downgrade their ratings shortly. (See CVNA stock forecast)

data.title: Sell Rivian Stock Because the R2 Won’t Save the Day, Says Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Rivian’s (NASDAQ:RIVN) upcoming mid-size SUV, the R2, is meant to be something of a game-changer for the EV maker, positioning it to become a real challenger to Tesla’s Model Y.

TipRanks Cyber Monday Sale

  • Claim 60% off TipRanks Premium for data-backed insights and research tools you need to invest with confidence.
  • Subscribe to TipRanks' Smart Investor Picks and see our data in action through our high-performing model portfolio - now also 60% off

However, one Street analyst is not convinced it will give the company – or the stock – the required boost. That is Morgan Stanley’s Andrew Percoco, who lays out the bear case. “While the headwinds around EV adoption are widely known, we see outsized risk for Rivian specifically heading into 2026 as they launch their next gen R2 vehicle,” Percoco, who ranks among the top 2% of Street stock experts, said.

Percoco believes the R2’s smaller form factor and lower price broaden its potential market and give buyers more options but thinks that it’s still hard to see the company fully making up for the loss of the $7,500 tax credit. At the same time, typical EV concerns – from range anxiety and charging access to worries about resale values, battery advances, and overall affordability – remain real challenges for a mass market vehicle.

To gauge the potential TAM for the R2, Percoco has looked at the size of the US midsize SUV segment. The top-selling mass-market midsize ICE models each move roughly 100,000–200,000 units a year, in what is a fragmented and competitive market. Specifically in the EV space, Tesla stands out, delivering about 370,000 Model Y units in the US in 2024. Percoco thinks Rivian is entering the market with a compelling product in terms of design and performance. Even so, he believes it will be challenging for the company to reach the kind of volume Tesla has with the Model Y unless Rivian’s autonomous-driving capabilities show “substantial acceleration,” especially with Tesla continuing to release more capable versions of its FSD system. “While we recognize that RIVN plans to compete in this area, with details forthcoming at its upcoming AI and autonomy day, we believe the capital requirements and time needed to be competitive may be longer than appreciated,” the 5-star analyst said on the matter.

Percoco assumes Rivian will grow R2 sales from 10,000 units in 2026 to 132,000 by 2030. That would amount to “substantial growth,” although it would still be well below Tesla’s current volume.

Additionally, while the R2 broadens Rivian’s addressable market, Percoco also thinks the company will face “residual-value pressure” from R1 lease returns, along with the risk of some demand being cannibalized. Moreover, although the company’s capital needs should be supported by the VW joint venture and the $6.6 billion DOE loan, Percoco believes Rivian is unlikely to generate positive operating cash flow before 2028 or positive FCF before 2030.

As such, with the prospect of an “EV winter and uncertain path to profitability,”Percoco assumed coverage of Rivian with a downgrade from Neutral to Underweight (i.e., Sell), although the $12 price target stays as is. Still, that figure sits 31% below the current share price. (To watch Percoco’s track record, click here)  

The Street’s average target is a more forgiving $13.98, although that still factors in a 12-month slide of 19.5%. All told, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating, based on a mix of 8 Holds and 5 Buys and Sells, each. (See RIVN stock forecast)

data.title: Tesla Stock: Here’s Why Morgan Stanley Has Moved to the Sidelines to “Wait for a Better Entry”
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is a company whose main business to date has been EV car sales, but famously its valuation wildly differs from other auto companies. That’s because it is viewed as more of a tech player, with other opportunities – particularly in AI – seen as up for grabs in the years ahead.

  • Claim 60% off TipRanks Premium for data-backed insights and research tools you need to invest with confidence.
  • Subscribe to TipRanks' Smart Investor Picks and see our data in action through our high-performing model portfolio - now also 60% off

But those opportunities don’t mean Tesla deserves unlimited credit. That appears to be the view of Morgan Stanley’s Andrew Percoco, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street. “Tesla is a clear global leader in electric vehicles, manufacturing, renewable energy, and real world AI and thus deserving of a premium valuation,” the 5-star analyst said. “However, high expectations on the latter have brought the stock closer to fair valuation.”

Although it’s widely recognized that Tesla operates well beyond traditional auto manufacturing, the analyst expects a “choppy trading environment” over the next year. Percoco sees potential downside to consensus estimates, and the key drivers tied to its non-auto segments seem fully valued at current levels. With the stock trading at 30x projected 2030 EBITDA (48x on MSe), with risks to near-term Street expectations, and with non-auto catalysts already priced in, Percoco thinks investors should “wait for a better entry.”

As such, having assumed coverage of the EV leader (taking over from Adam Jonas), Percoco has downgraded TSLA’s rating from Overweight (i.e., Buy) to Equal-weight (Neutral). Although Percoco has raised his price target from $410 to $425, the new figure sits 6.5% below the current share price. (To watch Percoco’s track record, click here)

Percoco’s new rating follows a full refresh to MS’s SOTP (sum-of-the-parts) valuation framework. That includes a lowered Tesla Auto valuation, now at $55 vs. $75 beforehand, because Percoco expects weaker volumes. His outlook includes a 10.5% cut to 2026 volumes and an 18.5% reduction in total deliveries through 2040, reflecting a more cautious view of the speed of EV adoption in the US and increasing global competition. This more conservative stance on the EV market means the MSe 2026 auto volume forecast sits 13% below consensus.

That new auto valuation is less than that of the new addition to the SOTP framework – the Humanoid business. Although the humanoid market is still in its early stages, Percoco thinks Tesla is well placed to lead given its manufacturing scale, vertical integration, data access, compute and energy capabilities, and its strong position in real-world AI. With that in mind, he now attributes $60 per share to Tesla’s Optimus segment.

Network Services, which includes FSD (full self-driving), is the “crown jewel,” and is given the lion’s share of the valuation – valued at $145, with Percoco believing its “leading-edge personal autonomous driving offering is a real game changer, and will remain a significant competitive advantage over its EV and non-EV peers.”

Tesla Mobility, which includes the Robotaxi business, comes next, valued at $125/share. Lastly, the Energy business is valued at $40/share.

Turning now to the rest of the Street, where based on a fairly even 11 additional Holds, 12 Buys, and 10 Sells, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating. At $383.54, the shares are expected to post a 12-month decline of 16%. (See Tesla stock forecast)

data.title: ‘All Airlines Are Poised to Bounce’: Citi Makes the Case for Buying These 2 Airline Stocks Now
data.description:
The airline industry struggled over the past year, with multiple headwinds weighing on demand. Operational disruptions, severe weather, shifting tariff policies, and – most recently – the government shutdown affecting the air traffic control system all contributed to weaker performance.

TipRanks Cyber Monday Sale

  • Claim 60% off TipRanks Premium for data-backed insights and research tools you need to invest with confidence.
  • Subscribe to TipRanks' Smart Investor Picks and see our data in action through our high-performing model portfolio - now also 60% off

Looking ahead to 2026, however, Citi analyst John Godyn sees conditions improving and expects a broad recovery across the sector. Still, not all airlines are positioned equally. Godyn highlights a group he refers to as the “supermajors” – the largest legacy carriers with entrenched advantages, including strong positions in key hubs, leadership on long-haul international routes, extensive alliance and joint-venture networks, and well-established loyalty programs. In Godyn’s view, these strengths should allow this group to be among the primary beneficiaries of the industry’s rebound.

 “We believe the setup is positive for an elongated mid-cycle beginning in 2026. If we are right, all airlines are poised to bounce. But we also argue that the biggest beneficiaries through the cycle are likely to be the supermajors, where risk/reward is most favorable relative to the rest, even if our directional view on the cycle proves wrong. By contrast, we see the legacy low-cost carriers (LLCCs) as the relative underperformers through the cycle. We are positively biased toward niche carriers and will revisit our ratings on a case-by-case basis,” Godyn explained.

The Citi’s airline expert goes on to recommend two airline stocks in particular, as shares that investors should buy now. We used the TipRanks database to see how the rest of the Street views them. Let’s dive in.

American Airlines Group (AAL)

The first stock on our list, American Airlines Group, is the parent company of American Airlines, one of the world’s largest commercial air carriers by number of passengers carried and by annual revenue. The company operates a fleet of approximately 1,000 modern airliners, from a variety of manufacturers: Airbus, Boeing, Bombardier, and Embraer. In addition, the company has nearly 300 aircraft on order, an impressive investment in keeping the fleet up to date.

American, operating in conjunction with its American Eagle regional partner air routes, offers thousands of flights every day to more than 350 destinations in over 60 countries around the world. The company has hubs in such major air travel destinations as New York City, Washington DC, Miami, LA, Chicago, and Philadelphia. These are supplemented by hubs in Charlotte, Phoenix, and Dallas-Fort Worth, where it maintains its headquarters.

In addition to its own operations, American Airlines is a member of the oneworld Alliance, a leading airline alliance in the world’s travel sector. Through the alliance, American cooperates with such major airlines as British Airways, Cathay Pacific Airways, and Qantas Airways; the airlines share facilities and gate privileges, provide support for each other’s passengers, and together provide travel options to over 900 destinations worldwide.

In 3Q25, the company reported $13.7 billion in quarterly revenues – a figure that marked a Q3 record for the company and beat the forecast by $62.9 million. At the same time, the company realized a net loss in the quarter of 17 cents per share, which was 11 cents per share better than had been expected. We should also note that AAL shares are down this year, having lost approximately 15% in 2025.

Despite the share price decline, Citi airline analyst John Godyn sees plenty of underlying strengths here. He writes of American, “Post COVID, poor execution at AAL led to lost corporate and premium travel market share, which led investors to question whether AAL was somehow structurally impaired vs. supermajor peers DAL and UAL, but management has increasingly put in plans to regain share. We believe that risks of structural impairment are overstated and that if AAL is able to demonstrate company-specific momentum then the stock offers the most significant upside potential among the supermajors. Our view in AAL’s upside potential is rooted in the following: (1) Sales and distribution ‘reset’ step in the right direction, (2) Premium hard-product refresh accelerating, (3) International expansion on a lower base can move the needle quickly, (4) Oneworld Alliance a major strategic asset, (5) Criticism of inferiority of network overblown, (6) Unique loyalty plan revenue opportunity, (7) Renewed focus on operational reliability, and (8) Productivity initiatives favorably impacting CASM ex-fuel profile further enhanced by labor cost visibility through 2027.”

Following from these comments, Godyn rates American Airlines’ stock as a Buy, with a price target of $19 that suggests a one-year upside potential of 28%. (To watch Godyn’s track record, click here)

Overall, AAL stock is rated as a Moderate Buy among Wall Street’s analysts, based on 13 recent reviews that include 5 Buys, 7 Holds and 1 Sell. The shares are priced at $14.81 and the $15 average target price implies that the stock will stay rangebound for the time being. (See AAL stock forecast)
